q1: Large language models are fundamentally next-token predictors built on the transformer architecture.
Pre-training trains on massive text corpora to instill broad text understanding.
Fine-tuning (including instruction tuning) shapes the model to follow instructions.
The two stages produce, in order, a base model and an instruction-following assistant.
q2: Transformers replace sequential recurrence with self-attention.
Self-attention is parallelizable across all positions in a sequence.
This parallelism enabled unprecedented scaling of neural networks for sequential data.
q3: Software 1.0 is explicit programming in languages like Python or C++.
Software 2.0 uses neural networks whose weights are learned from data.
The Software 2.0 development cycle centers on data curation and optimization rather than handwritten code.
AlphaGo is referenced as an example of the Software 2.0 paradigm.
q4: Auto-regressive LLMs are limited by their next-token-prediction objective.
LeCun proposes JEPA (Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture) as an alternative.
Objective-driven AI learns predictive world models in latent embedding space.
LeCun envisions AI as an amplifier of human intelligence, not a replacement.
q5: The popular perception of programming as fast, flawless typing is inaccurate.
The actual workflow is an iterative debug-fix cycle involving Google and Stack Overflow.
Debugging skill is the core productivity driver, not typing speed.
